2.28: vehaya acharei chen eshpoch et ruchi al kol basar venivve'u bneichem uvnotechem ziknechem chalomot yachalomun bachureichem cheziyonot yiru: 'And it shall come to pass afterward, that I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh; your sons and your daughters shall prophesy, your old men shall dream dreams, and your young men shall see visions.' The universal Spirit-outpouring prophesied in Joel 2:28-32 is the most significant single prophecy cited in the NT's account of Pentecost. The key phrase is kol basar (all flesh): unlike the selective, temporary Spirit-anointing of specific judges, prophets, and kings in the OT, the new covenant Spirit will be given to all — removing the mediatorial hierarchy that required prophets and priests to relay YHWH's word to the people. This is Jeremiah 31:34 ('no longer will each person teach his neighbor, for they will all know me') from the pneumatological angle.
2.1: tiqĕʿu shofar bĕTsiyon wĕhariʿu bĕhar qodshi: “Blow the trumpet in Zion; sound the alarm on my holy mountain!” The shofar (ram’s horn) was used for multiple purposes: warning of attack (Num 10:9; Neh 4:20), assembly for worship (Ps 81:3; Lev 25:9), and the eschatological trumpet (Isa 27:13; Matt 24:31; 1 Cor 15:52). Here it is the alarm of imminent invasion. Harʿiʿu (sound the alarm) — ruaʿ (to shout, blow an alarm; cf. the trumpet blasts at Jericho, Josh 6) — is the battle alarm distinct from the cultic trumpet blast. The combination of shofar + Zion + holy mountain positions this as both military and eschatological: the Day of the LORD is simultaneously a military invasion and a theophanic event.
2.2: yom choshekh waʾaphelah yom ʿanan waʾaraphel kĕshachar parash ʿal heharim: “A day of darkness and gloom, a day of clouds and thick darkness; like the dawn spreading over the mountains, there comes a great and mighty people.” The four darkness terms — choshekh (darkness), ʾaphelah (gloom, thick darkness), ʾanan (cloud), ʾaraphel (dense darkness, the cloud of theophany at Sinai: Exod 20:21; Deut 4:11) — accumulate to describe maximum obscurity. Kĕshachar parash ʿal heharim (like dawn spread over the mountains) — the simile is deliberately paradoxical: dawn is associated with light, but here it describes the locust army’s advance; the locusts are so dense they appear like the dark leading edge of sunrise spreading across the horizon.
2.3: lĕpanayw ʾakĕlah ʾesh wĕʾacharayw tĕlahet lahav: “Before them a fire devours, and behind them a flame burns; before them the land is like the garden of Eden, behind them a desolate wilderness.” The garden of Eden (gan ʿEden) appears here as the image of maximal agricultural fertility — the land at its pristine best. The locusts’ passage transforms Eden into a midbar shĕmamah (desolate wilderness). This garden-to-wilderness reversal is the theological anti-trajectory: creation moving backwards from ordered abundance toward primordial chaos. The same imagery will be reversed in the restoration of vv. 21-27.
2.4: kĕmarʾeh susim marʾehu ukĕpharashim ken yarutsun: “Their appearance is like that of horses; and like war horses, so they charge.” The locust-horse comparison is one of the most noted images in ancient natural history: the locust’s head does resemble a horse’s head (hence the German Heuschrecke = “hay horse” and the Italian cavalletta = little horse). The German and Italian terms suggest this comparison was widely observed across cultures. Revelation 9:7 applies the same imagery to the eschatological locust swarm from the abyss: “the locusts were like horses prepared for battle.”
2.5: kĕqol markavot ʿal rashĕ heharim yĕraqĕdun: “Like the rumbling of chariots they leap over the mountaintops.” The acoustic description of the locust army — chariot rumble, fire crackling through stubble, a mighty army set in battle array — captures what witnesses of large locust swarms describe: the sound of millions of locust wings produces a distinctive roar. Kĕqol lahav ʾesh ʾokhelah qash (like the crackling of a flame of fire consuming the stubble) — the locust’s consumption of vegetation produces a sound like fire running through dry grass. Kĕʿam ʿatsum ʿarukh milchamah (like a strong people set in array for battle) — the military metaphor is complete.
2.6: mippanayw yachilu ʿammim kol panim qibĕtsu phaʾrur: “Before them peoples writhe in anguish; every face turns pale.” Yachilu (writhe, be in anguish, from chul/chil = to writhe as in labor pains) — the pain of imminent judgment. Qibĕtsu phaʾrur (every face turns pale/draws in color) — phaʾrur is debated: it may mean “pot-color” (i.e., blackened, soot-colored; from a pot) or “glow” or “pallor.” The LXX reads chytra (pot). The MKT follows the majority reading of paleness/pallor as the most contextually appropriate response to terror.
2.7: kĕgibbourim yarutsun kĕʾanshei milchamah yaʿalu chomah: “Like warriors they run; like soldiers they scale the wall.” The military metaphor reaches its apex: the locusts scale walls like disciplined troops in a siege. Gibbourim (mighty men, warriors; cf. the gibbore chayil, the mighty men of valor) and ʾanshei milchamah (soldiers, men of war) are the two standard military designations. Wĕlo yĕʿavĕtun ʾorĕchotam (each keeps to his own course; they do not swerve from their paths) — the locusts’ disciplined advance is a marvel of natural history that observers consistently note: they move in one direction, parting around obstacles but not deviating from their course.
2.8: wĕʾish ʾachiv lo yidchaqun: “They do not push each other; each advances in his own track.” The discipline of the locust swarm continues: no internal conflict, no crowding, no jostling. Uvĕʿad hashelach yippolun lo yivtsaʿu (they burst through the weapons without being stopped) — shelach is a javelin or missile weapon; the locusts pass through defensive weaponry without injury. The untouchability of the army adds to the theological point: this is no ordinary enemy that can be repelled by military means.
2.9: baʿir yĕshoqĕqun baChomah yĕritsun babbatim yaʿalu bevʿad hachallonut yavoʾu kagannav: “They rush through the city; they run along the walls; they climb up into the houses; they enter through the windows like a thief.” The progressive penetration of the locust army from the city perimeter (rushing through the streets) to the walls to the houses to the windows mirrors the inexorable advance of an invading force that no fortification can stop. The comparison to a thief (kagannav) entering through windows anticipates the NT’s application of the thief-image to the coming of the Son of Man (Matt 24:43-44; 1 Thess 5:2) — the unexpected, unstoppable intrusion of judgment.
2.10: lĕpanayw ragĕzah ʾerets raʿashu shamayim shemesh wĕyareach qadaru wĕkochavim ʾasĕfu nogĕham: “Before them the earth quakes, the heavens tremble; the sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.” The locust army has now triggered cosmic disruption: earth quakes (ragaz), heavens tremble (raʿash), sun and moon darken. The cosmic signs of vv. 10-11 overlap with those of vv. 30-31 (the Spirit-outpouring oracle); Joel consistently links the locust plague with the eschatological Day of the LORD. The darkening of the sun and moon (from the density of the locust swarm) is simultaneously natural phenomenon and apocalyptic sign.
2.11: wĕYHWH natan qolo liphnei cheylo ki rav mĕʾod machanehu: “The LORD utters his voice before his army, for his forces are exceedingly great; he who executes his word is powerful.” YHWH is now explicitly identified as the commander of the locust army: cheylo (his army, his force). Natan qolo (uttered his voice) = YHWH’s thunder; the divine voice accompanies the theophany as in Ps 29 (“the voice of the LORD”) and Exod 19:16. Ki gadol yom YHWH wĕnora mĕʾod (for the day of the LORD is great and very terrible) — nora (awesome, fear-inducing, from yaraʾ) combines terror and reverence; the LXX renders it epiphanēs (glorious, manifest) — a significant divergence that the NT picks up.
2.12: wĕgam ʿattah nĕʾum YHWH shuvu ʿaday bĕkol lĕvavĕkhem uvĕtsom uvĕvchi uvĕmisped: “‘Yet even now,’ declares the LORD, ‘return to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping, and with mourning.’” Wĕgam ʿattah (yet even now) — the adversative introduces the divine grace window: despite the severity of the judgment just described, there is still time to return. Shuvu ʿaday (return to me) — shuv (to return, repent) is the prophetic word for repentance, expressing a turning of direction back toward YHWH. The three accompanying acts — fasting (tsom), weeping (bĕkhi), mourning (mispĕd) — are the external expressions of genuine internal contrition.
2.13: wĕqirĕʿu lĕvavĕkhem wĕʾal bigdĕkhem wĕshuvu ʾel YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “Rend your hearts and not your garments.” The command qirĕʿu lĕvavĕkhem (rend your hearts) takes the standard mourning gesture — tearing (qaraʿ) one’s garments (Gen 37:29; 2 Sam 1:11; Job 1:20) — and internalizes it: the heart must be torn, not merely the cloth. The external mourning gesture is not forbidden but is meaningless without the internal reality. The divine attributes formula that follows — channun wĕrachum ʾerekh ʾappayim wĕrav chesed wĕnicham ʿal haraʿah (gracious and merciful, slow to anger, and abounding in steadfast love, and relenting of disaster) — quotes Exodus 34:6-7, the “great confession” that is the OT’s most concentrated statement of YHWH’s character, repeated across the Hebrew Bible (Num 14:18; Neh 9:17; Ps 86:15; 103:8; 145:8; Jonah 4:2).
2.14: mi yodea yashuv wĕnicham wĕhishʾir ʾacharayw bĕrakah: “Who knows? He may turn and relent and leave behind a blessing.” Mi yodea (who knows?) is the formula of contingent hope — not expressing ignorance but opening a space of genuine possibility. The same formula appears at Jonah 3:9 (the Ninevites’ hope that God might relent) and 2 Samuel 12:22 (David’s fasting for the sick child). The possibility of YHWH leaving behind a blessing is expressed concretely: minchah wĕnesek lĕYHWH ʾeloheikhem (a grain offering and a drink offering for the LORD your God) — the very offerings whose cessation was mourned in 1:9 could be restored.
2.15: tiqĕʿu shofar bĕTsiyon qaddĕshu tsom qirʾu ʿatsarah: “Blow the trumpet in Zion; consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.” The shofar blast of v. 1 (alarm of invasion) is now redirected: the same instrument that announced judgment now summons the assembly for repentance. The trumpet has a double function in Joel — it both warns and calls to prayer. This verse echoes 1:14 almost exactly, but now the call comes after the description of the Divine Warrior’s army, making the urgency more acute.
2.16: ʾispu ʿam qaddĕshu qahal qibĕtsu zĕqenim ʾispu ʿolalim wĕyonĕqe shadayim: “Gather the people; consecrate the congregation; assemble the elders; gather the children and the nursing infants.” The assembly is comprehensively inclusive: elders (zĕqenim), children (ʿolalim), nursing infants (yonĕqe shadayim) — even those too young to fast or pray are brought to the assembly. The inclusion of the nursing infant is the most extreme extension: the entire community, including those incapable of understanding, is gathered before YHWH. Yetse chatan mechedro wĕchallah mechuppateha (let the bridegroom leave his bedroom and the bride her canopy) — even the newly married, normally exempt from military service and social obligations (Deut 24:5), must attend the communal fast.
2.17: bein haʾulam wĕlammizbeach yivku hakkohenim mĕshartei YHWH: “Between the vestibule (ʾulam) and the altar let the priests weep and say, ‘Spare your people, O LORD.’” The location — between the ʾulam (vestibule/porch of the temple) and the altar of burnt offering — is the specific priestly space of intercession in the temple court. Ezekiel 8:16 describes the abomination of men worshiping the sun in this same space, facing east; Joel restores the space to its proper use: priestly intercession facing YHWH. The prayer chus ʿal ʿammekha (spare your people) = have compassion; chus (to have pity, spare) is the visceral word for the compassion that withholds punishment.
2.18: wayĕqanneʾ YHWH lĕʾartso wayachmol ʿal ʿammo: “Then the LORD became zealous for his land and had compassion on his people.” The divine response: qannaʾ (jealous, zealous — the same root as the divine attribute “I the LORD your God am a jealous God,” Exod 20:5; Deut 4:24) and racham (had compassion, from rechem = womb; the deep maternal compassion). YHWH’s response to Israel’s repentance is characterized by the same intensity of emotion — jealousy/zeal for his land and people — that characterized his judgment.
2.19: wayaʿan YHWH wayoʾmer lĕʿammo hinnĕni sholeach lĕkhem ʾet hadagan wĕhattirosh wĕhayitshar: “The LORD answered and said to his people: ‘Behold, I am sending you grain, wine, and oil.’” YHWH’s answer to the priestly prayer (v. 17) is the restoration of the three Deuteronomic covenant blessings — dagan (grain), tirosh (new wine), and yitshar (oil) — exactly the three whose loss was mourned in 1:10. The reversal is complete and precise. Wĕloʾ ʾetten ʾetkhem ʿod cherpah baggoyim (I will no more make you a disgrace among the nations) — the shame of covenant curse (Deut 28:37) is removed.
2.20: wĕʾet hatsĕphoni ʾarchiq meʿaleikhem: “I will drive the northern invader far from you.” HaTsĕphoni (the northern one) — the locust army is now described geographically as coming from the north. Major locust migrations historically moved northeast-to-southwest across the Fertile Crescent; however, the “north” (tsaphon) also carried mythological significance in Canaanite religion as the home of Baal and the divine assembly (cf. Ps 48:2; Isa 14:13). The identification of the northern invader’s van into the eastern sea (Dead Sea) and rear into the western sea (Mediterranean) = complete expulsion from the land from east to west.
2.21: ʾal tiraʾi ʾadamah gili wĕsimchi ki higdil YHWH laʿasot: “Fear not, O land; be glad and rejoice, for the LORD has done great things.” The personified land (ʾadamah) that mourned in 1:10 is now commanded to rejoice. Higdil YHWH laʿasot (the LORD has done great things) — the language of the Psalms of praise (Ps 126:2-3: “The LORD has done great things for us; we are glad”). The reversal of mourning into joy is expressed through the same vocabulary in both directions.
2.22: ʾal tiraʾu behemot sadeh ki dasheʾu neʾot midbar ki ʿets nasaʾ piryo: “Fear not, you wild animals, for the pastures of the wilderness are green again.” The wild animals that groaned (1:18) and cried out to YHWH (1:20) are also addressed in the restoration. The wilderness pastures are green (dasheʾu, from desheʾ = fresh vegetation), the trees bear fruit, the fig tree and vine give full yield — exact reversals of the locust plague’s devastation.
2.23: uvnei Tsiyon gilu wĕsimchu bĕYHWH ʾeloheikhem ki natan lĕkhem ʾet hamoreh litsĕdaqah: “Be glad, O children of Zion, and rejoice in the LORD your God, for he has given the early rain as a sign of his faithfulness.” Hamoreh litsĕdaqah is the verse’s philological crux. Moreh has two possible meanings: (1) early rain (yoreh, the autumn rain) — which is the most natural reading in an agricultural context and fits the immediate verse (followed by “early rain and the spring rain”); (2) teacher — the Qumran community (Moreh haTsĕdaqah, the Teacher of Righteousness) understood this verse as a prophecy of their eschatological leader. Litsĕdaqah = either “in/according to righteousness” (modifying YHWH’s character) or “for righteousness” (the purpose of the teacher). The MKT follows the agricultural reading: “the early rain as a sign of his faithfulness” — tsĕdaqah here rendered as “faithfulness” in the covenantal sense (YHWH’s righteous keeping of his promises).
2.24: umaleʾu hagĕranot bar wĕheshiqu hayayqavim tirosh wĕyitshar: “The threshing floors shall be full of grain; the vats shall overflow with wine and oil.” The threshing floor (goren) and wine/oil vats (yeqev) are the loci of agricultural processing — the endpoints of the harvest cycle. Their overflowing fulfills the reversal of 1:17 (empty storehouses and ruined granaries). The language echoes the covenant blessing of Deuteronomy 28:8 (“The LORD will command the blessing on you in your barns and in all that you undertake”) and Proverbs 3:10 (“your barns will be filled with plenty, and your vats will be bursting with wine”).
2.25: wĕshillamti lĕkhem ʾet hashanim ʾasher ʾakhal haʾarbeh hayelek wĕhechasil wĕhagazam: “I will restore to you the years that the swarming locust has eaten.” YHWH promises to restore (shalam, the causative of shalom = to make complete, to repay) — the same root as shalom (wholeness/peace) and the concept of making restitution. The restoration covers “the years” — not just the current year’s harvest but the accumulated agricultural deficit. The four locust terms recur in reverse order (arbeh, yelek, chasil, gazam vs. 1:4’s gazam, arbeh, yelek, chasil), possibly signaling a deliberate reversal of the destruction sequence.
2.26: waʾakhaltem ʾakol wĕsavoaʿ uhillaltem ʾet shem YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “You shall eat in plenty and be satisfied, and praise the name of the LORD your God.” The covenant cycle of abundance → satisfaction → praise is the Deuteronomic ideal (Deut 8:10: “you shall eat and be full, and you shall bless the LORD your God”). Wĕloʾ yevoshu ʿammi lĕʿolam (my people shall never again be ashamed) — the shame (bosheth) of the covenant curse (Deut 28:37; cf. Joel 1:11) is permanently removed. This phrase is repeated in v. 27, creating a double assurance of the end of shame.
2.27: wiyĕdaʿtem ki bĕqerev Yisraʾel ʾani wĕʾani YHWH ʾeloheikhem wĕʾein ʿod: “You shall know that I am in the midst of Israel, and that I am the LORD your God and there is none else.” Wĕyĕdaʿtem (you shall know) — the recognition formula that the prophets use for the eschatological moment when YHWH’s identity becomes undeniable through his saving acts (Ezek’s “and they shall know that I am the LORD”). Wĕʾein ʿod (and there is none else) is the strict monotheistic formula of Deutero-Isaiah (Isa 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22; 46:9), here applied to YHWH’s presence among Israel. The agricultural restoration produces theological knowledge.
2.29: wĕgam ʿal haʿavadim wĕʿal hashaphachot bayyamim hahem ʾeshpoch ʾet ruchi: “Even on the male and female servants in those days I will pour out my Spirit.” ʿAvadim wĕshaphachot (male and female servants/slaves) — the extension of the Spirit gift to the lowest social stratum of ancient Israel makes the universality of v. 28 (“all flesh”) concrete: it includes even those with no social standing. The LXX and Peshitta preserve “my servants” and “my handmaidens” (which Peter quotes in Acts 2:18 as “my male servants and my female servants”), emphasizing that these slaves become servants of YHWH, not merely servants of their human masters.
2.30: wĕnatatti mophĕtim bashamayim uvʾarets dam wĕʾesh wĕtimrot ʿashan: “And I will show wonders in the heavens and on the earth: blood and fire and columns of smoke.” Mophĕtim (wonders, portents) — the same word used for the Egyptian plagues in Exodus (Exod 7:3; 11:9-10). The cosmic signs — blood (dam), fire (ʾesh), columns of smoke (timrot ʿashan, literally “palm-tree columns of smoke,” the vertical shape of a smoke column) — are associated with both theophany and warfare. The cosmic signs accompany the pouring of the Spirit, situating the Spirit-outpouring within the eschatological Day of the LORD.
2.31: hashemesh yehaphekh lĕchoshekh wĕhayareach lĕdam lifnei bo yom YHWH haggadol wĕhannora: “The sun shall be turned to darkness, and the moon to blood, before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.” Hashemesh yehaphekh lĕchoshekh (the sun turned to darkness) — haphak (to overturn, turn upside down; used for the overthrow of Sodom, Gen 19:25) describes a violent inversion. Hayayreach lĕdam (the moon to blood) — the blood-red moon describes a lunar eclipse or smoke-obscured moon during warfare or disaster. The phrase yom YHWH haggadol wĕhannora (the great and awesome Day of the LORD) = the intensified form of the Day formula, reaching its eschatological maximum. Peter quotes this verse (Acts 2:16-21) as inaugurated but not yet consummated at Pentecost.
2.32: wĕhayah kol ʾasher yiqraʾ bĕshem YHWH yimmalet: “And it shall come to pass that everyone who calls on the name of the LORD shall be saved.” The universality of kol ʾasher yiqraʾ (everyone who calls) matches the universality of “all flesh” in v. 28. Bishem YHWH (on the name of the LORD) — the Tetragrammaton; Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:13 and applies it to Jesus (Kyrios), making the most direct christological substitution of the divine Name in the NT: “because the same Lord is Lord of all, bestowing his riches on all who call on him” (Rom 10:12). Yimmalet (shall be saved/escape) from malat (to escape, be delivered) — the same root used for Lot’s escape from Sodom (Gen 19:22); salvation conceived as escape from destruction on the Day of YHWH.

1.1: dĕvar YHWH ʾasher hayah ʾel Yoʾel ben Pĕtuʾel: “The word of the LORD that came to Joel son of Pethuel.” Joel (Yoʾel) = “YHWH is God,” a theophoric name combining the divine names Yo (YHWH) and El. Pethuel is otherwise unknown; some MSS read “Bethuel” (the father of Rebekah, Gen 22:22). No king is mentioned in the superscription — unlike Hosea, Amos, Isaiah, and Micah — making Joel’s date one of the most disputed in the prophetic corpus. Proposals range from pre-exilic (9th century) to post-exilic (5th century BCE); the absence of Assyria and Babylon as named threats and the prominence of the temple cult favor a post-exilic setting for many scholars.
1.2: shimʿu zoʾt hazzĕqenim wĕhaʾazinu kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets: “Hear this, you elders; listen closely, all inhabitants of the land.” The dual summons formula (shimʿu... haʾazinu) opens the prophetic oracle with two imperatives from different roots: shamaʿ (to hear, attend) and ʾazan (to give ear, often used in poetry with shamaʿ; cf. Deut 32:1; Isa 1:2). Hazzĕqenim (the elders) are the communal leadership; kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets (all inhabitants of the land) includes everyone. The rhetorical question “Has anything like this ever happened in your days, or in the days of your fathers?” positions the locust plague as historically unprecedented — a hyperbole that signals divine rather than merely natural causation.
1.3: ʿaleha livneikhem sapperu uvneikhem livnehem uvnehem lĕdor ʾacher: “Tell your children about it, and let your children tell their children.” The chain of generational transmission (ʾav → ben → ben → dor ʾacher) is the same mechanism commanded for the Passover narrative (Exod 12:26-27; 13:8; Deut 6:20-21). The locust plague is to be memorialized as a foundational event — a theological benchmark of YHWH’s severity — transmitted through the same oral tradition that carried Israel’s saving history. The urgency of transmission signals that Joel is not describing a natural disaster but a revelation of YHWH’s character and purpose.
1.4: yeter hagazam ʾakal haʾarbeh wĕyeter haʾarbeh ʾakal hayelek wĕyeter hayelek ʾakal hechasil: “What the cutting locust left, the swarming locust has eaten; what the swarming locust left, the hopping locust has eaten; and what the hopping locust left, the stripping locust has eaten.” This verse is the philological crux of Joel 1. Four Hebrew terms are used: gazam (root: to cut, shear), arbeh (the most common word for locust, from rabah = to be many/swarm), yelek (from a root meaning to lick or hop), and chasil (from chasal = to consume, destroy). The dominant scholarly debate: (1) four different species of locust with slightly different habits; (2) four stages of locust development (egg → larva → nymph → adult); (3) poetic synonyms emphasizing total devastation through accumulation. The progression “what the first left, the second ate” suggests either sequential waves of infestation or a merism: the totality of destruction across all locust varieties and stages. The MKT renders each term distinctively (cutting, swarming, hopping, stripping) to preserve the sense of accumulating destruction without committing to a developmental sequence.
1.5: hiqitsu shikkorim uvku wehililu kol shote yayin ʿal ʿasis: “Wake up, you drunkards, and weep! Wail, all you wine drinkers, because of the sweet wine — it has been cut off from your lips.” Shikkorim (drunkards, from shakar = to become drunk) are the first audience addressed — those who depend on wine most will feel the loss most immediately. ʿAsis is specifically sweet wine or pressed juice, possibly unfermented grape juice or must; the LXX translates it gleukos (sweet wine/new wine, the same word used in Acts 2:13 when the crowd accuses the Spirit-filled disciples of being full of gleukos). The irony of calling drunkards to weep is deliberate: those who sought pleasure in wine must now mourn its absence.
1.6: ki goy ʿalah ʿal artsi ʿatsum wĕʾen mispar: “For a nation has invaded my land — powerful and beyond counting.” The locusts are personified as a goy (nation, people) — the same word used for Israel’s enemies. ʿAtsum (powerful, mighty) and ʾen mispar (without number, innumerable) = the classic description of an overwhelming army. Shinnayw kĕshinne ʾaryeh (its teeth like lion’s teeth) and mĕtallĕʿot lavi lo (the fangs of a lioness) apply the two forms of the Hebrew lion — ʾaryeh (lion) and lavi (lioness, the more ferocious) — to describe the locust’s destructive power as predatory rather than merely consumptive.
1.7: sam gapni lĕshamah utĕʾenati liqĕtsafah: “It has stripped my vine bare and splintered my fig tree.” The vine (gephen) and fig tree (teʾenah) are the emblems of covenantal prosperity (1 Kgs 4:25; Mic 4:4; Zech 3:10). Their destruction is not merely economic but symbolically theological: the covenant blessings are being reversed. Liqĕtsafah (stripped bare, peeled; some read “splintered”) is a strong term — the tree is not merely stripped of fruit but its bark is peeled off, leaving it white and dead. The MKT’s “splintered” emphasizes the totality of destruction.
1.8: qoni kĕvulat saqqim ʿal baʿal nĕʿureha: “Mourn like a young woman dressed in sackcloth, grieving for the husband of her youth.” Bĕtulah (young woman, virgin) in context here means a young bride whose husband has died; the MKT’s “husband of her youth” follows the MT’s baʿal nĕʿureha (lord/husband of her youth). The mourning gesture — saqqim (sackcloth, rough goat-hair garment worn against the skin as penitential discipline; cf. Gen 37:34; 2 Sam 3:31; Jonah 3:5) — clothes the body in the coarsest material as an expression of extreme grief.
1.9: nichrat minchah wĕnesek mibbeit YHWH: “The grain offering and the drink offering are cut off from the house of the LORD.” Nichrat (is cut off, from karat = to cut) — the same root used for covenant-cutting (karat bĕrit) and for capital punishment. The cessation of the daily minchah (grain offering) and nesek (drink offering) — the tamid, the continuous sacrifice prescribed in Exod 29:38-42 — signals the breakdown of the entire cultic system. Without grain and wine, the temple cult cannot function; YHWH’s house is in mourning with the land.
1.10: shadad sadeh ʾavĕlah ʾadamah: “The fields are ruined; the soil is in mourning.” Shadad (ruined, devastated) is a violent verb — used for military destruction of cities; the locust plague is linguistically assimilated to an enemy invasion. ʾAvĕlah ʾadamah (the ground mourns) — the land itself is a moral actor, mourning as a person mourns; cf. Isa 24:4; Jer 12:4; Hos 4:3. The three agricultural staples — dagan (grain), tirosh (new wine/fresh grape juice), and yitshar (olive oil) — are the basic triad of Deuteronomic blessing (Deut 7:13; 11:14; 12:17). Their failure = the covenant curse reversed.
1.11: hobishu ʾikarim helilu korem ʿal chitah wĕʿal sĕʿorah ki ʾavad qetsir sadeh: “Be ashamed, O farmers; wail, O vinedressers — for the wheat and for the barley — because the harvest of the field is lost.” Hobishu (be ashamed, from bosh) = the shame of those who planted expecting a harvest and received devastation instead. The specific mention of chitah (wheat) and sĕʿorah (barley) — the two principal grain crops of ancient Israel — grounds the judgment in agricultural reality. Barley ripened in spring (March-April), wheat in early summer (May-June); the locust plague consuming both means a total agricultural failure.
1.12: hayĕvĕshah hagephen wĕʾumĕlalah tĕʾenah: “The vine has dried up and the fig tree withers.” The catalogue of perishing trees — rimon (pomegranate), tamar (date palm), tapuach (apple or apricot), “all the trees of the field” — is a comprehensive inventory of orchard agriculture. Hevishah sason min bĕne ʾadam: “joy has withered away from the sons of men” — sason (joy, gladness) personified as something that has itself dried up like a tree. The agricultural devastation produces emotional devastation; the land’s fruitfulness and human flourishing are inseparably linked.
1.13: chĕgoru wĕsipdu hakkohenim helilu mĕshartei mizbĕach: “Gird yourselves and lament, O priests; wail, O ministers of the altar.” Chĕgoru saqqim (gird on sackcloth) is the sacerdotal penitential act: the priests themselves must put on the mourners’ garment. Bou linu bassaqqim (come, spend the night in sackcloth) — the overnight vigil in sackcloth intensifies the mourning; night is when the penitent is most vulnerable and the prayer most desperate (cf. Ps 22:2; Lam 2:19). The priests are called to lead the national mourning in their capacity as intercessors between Israel and YHWH.
1.14: qaddĕshu tsom qirʾu ʿatsarah ʾisphu zĕqenim kol yoshĕve ha-ʾarets beit YHWH ʾeloheikhem: “Consecrate a fast; call a solemn assembly.” Qiddash tsom (consecrate a fast) — fasting was consecrated as a sacred act of corporate humiliation before YHWH (cf. 2 Chr 20:3; Ezra 8:21; Neh 9:1). ʿAtsarah (solemn assembly, from a root meaning to restrain/hold back) = a day of cessation from normal activity for religious assembly; used particularly for the closing assembly of the major festivals (Lev 23:36; Num 29:35). The gathering at “the house of the LORD your God” positions the temple as the center of national repentance.
1.15: ʾahah layom ki qarov yom YHWH ukĕshod mishshaddai yavoʾ: “Alas for the day! For the day of the LORD is near, and it comes as devastation from the Almighty.” Yom YHWH (the Day of the LORD) appears five times in Joel (1:15; 2:1, 11, 31; 3:14) — more than in any other single prophetic book. Mishshaddai (from Shaddai, the Almighty) — the divine epithet El Shaddai is archaic, associated with the patriarchal traditions (Gen 17:1; 28:3). Joel creates a wordplay: kĕshod mishshaddai (as devastation from Shaddai) — shod (destruction, devastation) and Shaddai share consonants, suggesting that the name of the Almighty contains within it the threat of destruction. The LXX renders Shaddai as Pantokrator (All-Ruler), losing the wordplay.
1.16: halo neghed ʿeinenu ʾokhel nichrat mibbeit ʾeloheinu simchah wagil: “Is not the food already cut off before our eyes, and joy and gladness from the house of our God?” The rhetorical question confirms that the judgment is already underway — it is not future but present reality. Simchah wagil (joy and gladness) from the house of God = the festive worship that should accompany the agricultural harvest celebrations (Deut 16:11, 14: “rejoice before the LORD your God” at the Feast of Weeks and Feast of Booths) — these are silenced along with the grain and wine.
1.17: ʿavĕshu pĕrudot tachat mĕgrĕphoteihem: “The seeds have shrivelled in the ground.” ʿAvĕshu pĕrudot is one of the most philologically difficult phrases in Joel: ʿavash (to shrivel, wither) appears here uniquely; pĕrudot may mean “seeds” or possibly “grain” (from parad = to separate, hence separated/individual kernels). The LXX and other versions show uncertainty. Mĕgrĕphoteihem (their shovels/clods) = the tools used for planting or the clods of earth. The storehouses (mamĕgurot) are empty and the granaries (ʾasomot) broken down — the entire infrastructure of food storage has collapsed.
1.18: mah neʾĕnechah bĕhemah navoku ʿadrei vaqar: “How the animals groan! The herds of cattle are confused because there is no pasture for them.” Neʾĕnechah (they groan, from ʾanach = to sigh, groan) — the same root applied to Israel’s groaning under Egyptian slavery (Exod 2:23). The animal world shares in the covenant consequences of human sin; the groaning of animals becomes a kind of wordless prayer (cf. Ps 104:21; Job 38:41). Navoku (are confused, disorientated, from navak = to wander in confusion) — the same root used for Israel in the wilderness and for Pharaoh’s army at the Red Sea (Exod 14:3). The cattle’s confusion mirrors Israel’s own disorientation before YHWH’s judgment.
1.19: ʾeleikha YHWH ʾeqraʾ ki ʾesh ʾakĕlah neʾot midbar: “To you, O LORD, I cry out. For fire has devoured the open pastures.” The shift from description (vv. 2-18) to direct address (ʾeleikha YHWH ʾeqraʾ) marks the transition to lament prayer — the genre of Psalms 22, 44, 88. ʾEsh ʾakĕlah (fire has devoured) — the “fire” alongside the locusts likely refers to the drought’s scorching heat, which dried up the pastures before the locusts consumed what remained; or possibly literal fire sparked by the drought conditions. The open pastures (neʾot midbar, the wilderness pasturelands) and “all the trees of the field” (ʿets hassadeh) encompass both cultivated and uncultivated land.
1.20: gam bĕhemot sadeh taʾarog ʾeleikha: “Even the wild animals pant and cry to you, for the water brooks have dried up.” Taʾarog (pants/longs, from ʾarag) = the same verb used in Psalm 42:1 (“as the deer pants for flowing streams, so my soul pants for you, O God”) — the universal longing for YHWH expressed through the animal kingdom’s thirst. The dry ʾaphiqe mayim (water channels/brooks) and the fire consuming the wilderness pastures together describe a total environmental collapse: both water and vegetation have failed. Joel’s ecological vision encompasses the full scope of creation’s suffering when the covenant is broken.

3.1: ki hinneh bayyamim hahem uvʾet hahi ʾasher ʾashiv ʾet shĕvut Yĕhudah wĕYĕrushalayyim: “For behold, in those days and at that time, when I restore the fortunes of Judah and Jerusalem.” Shiv ʾet shĕvut (restore the fortunes) — a formulaic phrase of eschatological restoration used across the prophetic corpus (Jer 30:3; 33:7; Ezek 39:25; Amos 9:14). The phrase literally means “turn the turning/captivity” — whether shĕvut comes from shavah (captivity) or shuv (returning) is debated; the MKT follows the majority reading of “restore the fortunes.” The temporal marker “in those days and at that time” links ch. 3 to the Spirit-outpouring of 2:28-32, placing all of ch. 3 in the eschatological horizon.
3.2: wĕqibbatsthi ʾet kol haggoyim wĕhoridat im ʾel ʾemeq Yĕhoshaphat: “I will gather all the nations and bring them down to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; and I will enter into judgment with them there.” ʾEmeq Yĕhoshaphat (Valley of Jehoshaphat) — the name means “YHWH judges” (YHWH + shaphat = to judge). This is the only occurrence of this place name in the Hebrew Bible, and it was likely a theological name for the site of eschatological judgment rather than an existing geographic location. Later Christian tradition identified it with the Kidron Valley between Jerusalem and the Mount of Olives; 4th-century CE sources reflect this identification. ʿAl ʿammi wĕnachalati Yisraʾel (on behalf of my people and my heritage Israel) — the nations are judged specifically for their treatment of Israel, YHWH’s nachalah (inheritance, heritage).
3.3: wĕʾet ʿammi yarvu laggoral wĕyittĕnu hayyeled bazzanah wĕhayaldah maĕru bayyin wayyishthu: “For my people they have cast lots; they have traded a boy for a prostitute, and sold a girl for wine and drunk it.” The slave trade described — trading human beings for a prostitute’s services or for wine — reflects ancient Near Eastern slave markets following military conquest. The dehumanization of Israel’s children to the level of commodities exchangeable for a single night’s entertainment is the specific indictment against the nations. Casting lots (yarvu goral) for slaves was standard practice after conquest (cf. Nahum 3:10; Obadiah 11).
3.4: wĕgam mah ʾattem li Tsor wĕTsidon wĕkol gĕlilot Pĕleshet: “But you — what are you to me, O Tyre and Sidon, and all the regions of Philistia?” The rhetorical question mah ʾattem li (what are you to me?) expresses divine contempt — the same formula in Jeremiah 2:18 and John 2:4. Tyre, Sidon, and Philistia are specifically accused because they were the slave-trading intermediaries who profited from Judah’s conquest. ʾIm gĕmul ʾattem meshalĕmim ʿalay (if you are paying me back for something) — the nations are challenging YHWH through their treatment of Israel; they have made the conflict personal.
3.5: ʾasher kaspi wĕzahavi lĕqachthem wĕmachemadai hatovim haveʾtem lĕheikhaleikem: “For you have taken my silver and my gold, and have carried my rich treasures into your temples.” The silver and gold of Israel are YHWH’s silver and gold — the possessive frames the plundering of Israel as robbery from YHWH himself. Heikhaleikem (your temples/palaces) — the heikal (great hall) refers to either the inner sanctuary of a temple or a royal palace; in both cases the Israelite valuables have been incorporated into the cult or treasury of the foreign deity. The plundering of sacred objects was a declaration that the victor’s god had defeated the loser’s god.
3.6: uvĕnei Yĕhudah uvĕnei Yĕrushalayyim mĕkartem livĕnei hayyĕvanim: “You have sold the people of Judah and Jerusalem to the Greeks (Yĕvanim).” Yĕvanim (Greeks) = Ionians, the Greek-speaking peoples (cf. Isa 66:19; Ezek 27:13 where Javan appears in trade lists). The sale of Judean slaves to the Greeks is historically documented: Greek slave markets received captives from Phoenician traders throughout the eastern Mediterranean from at least the 8th century BCE. This verse is one of the arguments for a post-exilic date for Joel: Greek slave trade with the Levant was particularly prominent in the 5th-4th centuries BCE.
3.7: hinnĕni mĕʿiram min hammaqqom ʾasher mĕkartem ʾotam sham: “Behold, I will rouse them from the place to which you sold them.” YHWH announces a reversal of the slave trade: those who were sold to distant places will be raised up and returned. Wĕhashivoti gĕmulĕkhem bĕroʾshĕkhem (I will return your payment on your own heads) — the principle of lex talionis applied to national commerce: what you did to Israel will be done to you, bĕroʾshĕkhem (on your own heads), the Hebrew idiom for personal accountability (cf. Ezek 16:43; 1 Kgs 2:44).
3.8: umakharti ʾet bĕneikhem uvĕnoteikem bĕyad bĕnei Yĕhudah: “I will sell your sons and your daughters into the hands of the people of Judah, and they will sell them to the Sabeans.” The Sabeans (Shĕvaʾim) = the people of Sheba in southern Arabia (modern Yemen), known in the ancient world as long-distance traders (cf. 1 Kgs 10; Job 6:19; Isa 60:6). The reversal is exact: those who sold Judeans to distant traders will themselves be sold to a distant trading nation — the eschatological reversal of the slave trade that mirrors the prophetic pattern of nations receiving what they inflicted on Israel (cf. Obadiah; Nahum).
3.9: qirĕʾu zoʾt baggoyim qaddĕshu milchamah haʿiru haggibbourim yiggeshu yaʿalu kol ʾanshei hammilchamah: “Proclaim this among the nations: Prepare for war! Rouse the mighty men!” Qaddĕshu milchamah (consecrate/prepare for war) — the verb qiddash (to sanctify, consecrate) is applied to war: holy war in the ANE involved cultic preparation, including animal sacrifice, divination, ritual purity, and prophetic consultation before battle. Joel ironically applies this sacred vocabulary to the nations’ mobilization for the battle they will lose. The military roster — gibbourim (mighty men), ʾanshei hammilchamah (men of war) — assembles the full military force of the nations for YHWH’s tribunal.
3.10: kuttu ʾitĕkhem lĕcharavot wĕmizmaroteikhem lirĕmachim: “Beat your plowshares into swords and your pruning hooks into spears.” This is the deliberate reversal of Isaiah 2:4/Micah 4:3: “they shall beat their swords into plowshares, and their spears into pruning hooks.” Joel 3:10 inverts the eschatological peacetime vision of the other prophets: YHWH is calling the nations to war precisely so that he can defeat them. The plowshare (ʾet) and pruning hook (mazmerah) are transformed into sword (cherev) and spear (romach). Heʾanis ʾemar gibbor ʾani (let the weak say “I am a warrior”) — even those unqualified for battle are summoned; no one is exempted from YHWH’s court.
3.11: ʿushu wĕvoʾu kol haggoyim miSaviv wĕqibĕtsu shanah: “Hasten and come, all you surrounding nations, and gather yourselves there.” The double summons (ʿush = hasten; vavoʾ = come) creates urgency; YHWH is calling the nations to their own judgment. Hanchit YHWH gibboureikhah (bring down your warriors, O LORD) — a sudden prayer directed to YHWH in the middle of the summons to the nations; the speaker (the prophet? Judah?) calls on YHWH to deploy his own heavenly army (gibbourim). The LXX renders this differently, and the MT is difficult; the MKT follows the MT reading.
3.12: yeʿoru wĕyaʿalu haggoyim ʾel ʿemeq Yĕhoshaphat ki sham ʾeshev lishpot ʾet kol haggoyim missaviv: “Let the nations be roused and come up to the Valley of Jehoshaphat; for there I will sit to judge all the surrounding nations.” ʾEshev lishpot (I will sit to judge) — YHWH seated as judge (shaphat) is the formal posture of judicial authority in the ancient world (cf. Ps 9:4, 7; Isa 16:5). The Valley of Jehoshaphat (“YHWH judges”) is where YHWH sits to judge — the name and the action are the same: the judgment-place is where YHWH-who-judges presides.
3.13: shilchu magal ki basal qatsir boʾu redu ki malʾah gat shiqqi gĕdĕshu: “Put in the sickle, for the harvest is ripe. Go in and tread, for the winepress is full.” The two harvest images — grain harvest (sickle, magal) and grape harvest (winepress, gat) — are used for the harvest of nations in judgment. The sickle in the harvest appears in Revelation 14:14-20, where the Son of Man harvests the earth and the angel harvests the grapes of wrath. Ki rabbah raʿatam (for their wickedness is great) — the fullness of the winepress = the fullness of the nations’ wickedness, which has reached the point requiring divine action.
3.14: hamonim hamonim bĕʿemeq hecharuts ki qarov yom YHWH bĕʿemeq hecharuts: “Multitudes, multitudes in the valley of decision! For the day of the LORD is near in the valley of decision.” Hamonim hamonim (multitudes, multitudes) — the doubled noun for emphasis; the same root as hamon (noise, tumult, crowd) that runs through Ezekiel’s oracles against the nations. ʿEmeq hecharuts (valley of decision/verdict) — charutz means “incised, decisive, sharp” (from charats = to cut, determine); the “decision” is YHWH’s verdict, not human free choice — the valley is named for the decisiveness of the divine judgment that takes place there. The repetition of the phrase creates a solemn cadence marking the climax of the Day of the LORD.
3.15: shemesh wĕyareach qadaru wĕkochavim ʾasĕfu nogĕham: “The sun and the moon grow dark, and the stars withdraw their shining.” The cosmic darkness of 2:10 is reprised at the eschatological climax: the same cosmic signs surround both the locust invasion (which introduces the Day of the LORD) and the final judgment of the nations. This repetition reinforces Joel’s typological structure: the locust invasion is a historical type of the final Day of the LORD, and its cosmic accompaniments prefigure the final cosmic upheaval.
3.16: wĕYHWH miTsiyon yishʾag umirushalayyim yitten qolo wĕraʿashu shamayim waʾarets: “The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the heavens and the earth shake.” This verse is nearly identical to Amos 1:2 (“The LORD roars from Zion and utters his voice from Jerusalem; the pastures of the shepherds mourn...”), suggesting a shared prophetic tradition. Yishʾag (roars, from shaʾag = the roar of a lion) positions YHWH as the lion whose roar terrorizes the nations (cf. Amos 3:8). Wĕmachseh livĕnei Yisraʾel (but the LORD is a refuge for his people, a stronghold for the people of Israel) — the same divine action that destroys the nations is the protection of Israel: YHWH’s terrifying roar is simultaneously the war-cry that defeats the enemy and the lion’s roar that protects the pride.
3.17: widaʿtem ki ʾani YHWH ʾeloheikhem shoken bĕTsiyon har qodshi: “So you shall know that I am the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion, my holy mountain.” The recognition formula (wĕyĕdaʿtem ki ʾani YHWH) reaches its eschatological conclusion: the nations’ defeat produces Israel’s knowledge of YHWH. YHWH ʾeloheikhem shoken bĕTsiyon (the LORD your God, who dwells in Zion) — divine indwelling (shakan, the root of shekinah) in Zion as the eschatological reality; cf. Ezekiel 48:35 (“the LORD is there”) and Revelation 21:3 (“the dwelling of God is with man”). Wĕyĕrushalayyim tihyeh qodesh (Jerusalem shall be holy) = the consecration of the city that makes it truly the city of YHWH.
3.18: wĕhayah bayom hahu yittĕfu heharim ʿasis wĕhagĕvaʿot telnah chalav: “And in that day the mountains shall drip with sweet wine, and the hills shall flow with milk.” The eschatological abundance — mountains dripping wine (ʿasis), hills flowing with milk, all the wadis of Judah flowing with water — is the anti-image of the locust devastation of ch. 1. Wĕmayon mibbeit YHWH yetse wĕhishqah ʾet nachal haShittim (a fountain shall come forth from the house of the LORD and water the Wadi of Shittim) — the life-giving stream flowing from the temple is a key eschatological image: Ezekiel 47:1-12 develops it most fully; Zechariah 14:8 and Revelation 22:1-2 carry it into the NT’s vision of the New Jerusalem. Shittim was the last Israelite camp before crossing into Canaan (Num 25:1) — a site of apostasy and death; the fountain from YHWH’s house brings life even to that place of failure.
3.19: Mitsrayim lishĕmamah tihyeh wĕʾEdom lĕmidbar shĕmamah tihyeh: “Egypt shall become a desolation and Edom a desolate wilderness.” Egypt and Edom are named as the paradigmatic enemies of Israel: Egypt (the house of slavery) and Edom (the brother-nation that turned against Judah at the time of Jerusalem’s fall; cf. Ps 137:7; Obadiah 10-14). The specific charge — meias chamas livĕnei Yĕhudah ʾasher shaphĕku dam naqiʾ bĕʾartsam (for the violence done to the people of Judah, in whose land they shed innocent blood) — grounds the national judgment in specific historical crimes. Dam naqiʾ (innocent blood) is the standard phrase for unjust killing that cries out for divine vengeance (Gen 4:10; Deut 19:10; Jer 19:4).
3.20: wĕYĕhudah lĕʿolam teshev wĕYĕrushalayyim lĕdor wador: “But Judah shall be inhabited forever, and Jerusalem to all generations.” The eschatological permanence of Judah and Jerusalem stands in absolute contrast to the desolation of Egypt and Edom. Lĕʿolam (forever) and lĕdor wador (to all generations) — the two Hebrew expressions for permanence. This is the positive fulfillment of the Abrahamic and Davidic covenants: the land shall be possessed forever (Gen 17:8; 2 Sam 7:16). The NT rereads “Jerusalem” and “Zion” in terms of the heavenly Jerusalem (Gal 4:26; Heb 12:22; Rev 21).
3.21: wĕniqqeti damam lo niqqeti wĕYHWH shoken bĕTsiyon: “I will avenge their blood — the blood I have not yet avenged — for the LORD dwells in Zion.” The final verse of Joel is syntactically difficult: wĕniqqeti damam lo niqqeti may mean (1) “I will cleanse/avenge their blood which I have not yet cleansed/avenged” (taking niqqah as “to avenge”); or (2) “I will declare innocent their blood which I have declared not innocent” (treating the statement as a declaration of innocence for Israel’s shed blood). The MKT follows reading (1): YHWH promises to execute vengeance for the innocent blood shed against his people — the eschatological justice that the historical present has not yet seen. The book closes with the ground of hope: wĕYHWH shoken bĕTsiyon (the LORD dwells in Zion) — YHWH’s presence in Zion is the guarantee of his people’s future.
